---
title: "Chapter 18 — ⚡ Attention & Transformers"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 17 · 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models](ch17.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [19 · 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning →](ch19.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Almost every modern AI system you have heard of — large language models, image generators, protein-structure predictors — rests on a single architecture: the **transformer**. Its core idea is **attention**, a mechanism that lets each piece of a sequence look directly at every other piece and decide what is relevant. This chapter explains how attention works mechanically, how transformer blocks stack it into deep networks, and why this design displaced the recurrent models that came before it.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep Learning · the backbone of LLMs, modern vision, and multimodal models · every token directly attends to every other token, so context is gathered in one parallel step instead of being passed along a chain.
:::

## 18.1 — The attention intuition

Imagine reading the sentence "The animal didn't cross the street because *it* was too tired." To resolve what *it* refers to, your eyes flick back to "animal." You do not re-read the whole sentence linearly; you selectively pull the one word that matters. **Attention** is the formalization of exactly this move: for each element in a sequence, compute a weighted blend of all the other elements, where the weights say how relevant each one is.

Older sequence models — recurrent networks — instead squeezed all prior context into a single fixed-size hidden state that got updated one step at a time. Information about "animal" had to survive being overwritten across every intervening word before reaching "it." Attention removes that bottleneck: "it" can reach back to "animal" directly, in one hop, no matter how far apart they sit.

The key reframing is to treat the operation as a **soft, differentiable dictionary lookup**. A normal dictionary takes a key, finds the one exact match, and returns its value. Attention takes a query, compares it against *all* keys, and returns a blend of *all* values weighted by how well each key matched. Because the blend is smooth rather than a hard pick, the whole thing is differentiable and can be trained by gradient descent.

The picture below shows the three roles at a single glance: one query on the left fans out to compare against every key, the comparisons become weights, and those weights blend the values into a single output.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12">
  <rect x="10" y="85" width="60" height="30" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="40" y="104" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">query</text>
  <rect x="160" y="20" width="50" height="24" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <rect x="160" y="55" width="50" height="24" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <rect x="160" y="90" width="50" height="24" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="185" y="36" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">key₁</text>
  <text x="185" y="71" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">key₂</text>
  <text x="185" y="106" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">key₃</text>
  <line x1="70" y1="100" x2="160" y2="32" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.6"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="100" x2="160" y2="67" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="100" x2="160" y2="102" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="115" y="150" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor" font-style="italic">weights = softmax(q·kᵢ)</text>
  <rect x="280" y="55" width="60" height="24" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="310" y="71" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">values</text>
  <rect x="360" y="85" width="50" height="30" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="385" y="104" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">out</text>
  <line x1="340" y1="67" x2="360" y2="97" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
</svg>

::: {.callout-tip}
The mental model that unlocks everything in this chapter: attention is **content-based retrieval**. Each position asks a question (query), every position advertises what it offers (key) and what it would contribute (value), and the answer is a relevance-weighted average of those contributions.
:::

## 18.2 — Query, key, value and scaled dot-product attention

The dictionary analogy becomes concrete through three learned projections. Start with a sequence of input vectors (one per token). From each input vector $x$ we produce three different vectors by multiplying by three learned weight matrices:

- the **query** $q = x W^Q$ — "what am I looking for?"
- the **key** $k = x W^K$ — "what do I contain, as an advertisement?"
- the **value** $v = x W^V$ — "what will I actually pass on if attended to?"

To score how much one position should attend to another, we take the **dot product** of the asking position's query with the candidate position's key. A large dot product means the query and key point in similar directions — a strong match. We then turn the row of scores into a probability distribution with **softmax** (non-negative weights that sum to one) and use it to average the values.

Stacking all queries into a matrix $Q$, all keys into $K$, and all values into $V$, the entire operation is one compact formula:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

Here $d_k$ is the dimension of each key/query vector. The product $QK^\top$ is the matrix of all pairwise scores; softmax is applied row-wise; the result multiplies $V$ to produce the blended outputs.

**Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** If query and key entries are roughly independent with unit variance, their dot product over $d_k$ dimensions has variance $\approx d_k$, so the raw scores grow with dimension. Feed large-magnitude scores into softmax and it saturates: one weight goes to nearly 1, the rest to nearly 0, and the gradient through softmax vanishes. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ rescales the scores back to unit variance, keeping softmax in a responsive, well-gradiented regime. It is a small detail with outsized importance for stable training.

```python
import numpy as np

def attention(Q, K, V):
    # Q,K: (n, d_k)   V: (n, d_v)
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)              # (n, n) pairwise match scores
    scores -= scores.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)  # numerical stability
    w = np.exp(scores)
    w /= w.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)            # softmax over each row
    return w @ V                                 # blend of values

# sanity check: with near-hard weights, each query recovers its matching value
Q = np.eye(3); K = np.eye(3); V = np.array([[10.,0],[0,20.],[0,0]])
out = attention(Q*5, K, V)        # scale up so softmax is near one-hot
assert np.allclose(out, V, atol=0.5)
```

::: {.callout-warning}
The scaling factor is $\sqrt{d_k}$, not $d_k$. Dividing by $d_k$ over-shrinks the scores and flattens the attention toward a uniform average, which destroys the model's ability to focus. The square root is what matches the standard deviation of the dot product, not its variance.
:::

## 18.3 — Self-attention vs cross-attention

The formula above does not care where $Q$, $K$, and $V$ come from. Two important configurations arise depending on the source.

In **self-attention**, all three are projected from the *same* sequence. Every token attends to every token in its own sequence, including itself. This is how a model builds context-aware representations: the vector for "it" gets enriched with information pulled from "animal," so that after the attention layer, "it" no longer means a generic pronoun but specifically *this animal in this sentence*.

In **cross-attention**, the queries come from one sequence while the keys and values come from *another*. This is the bridge between two different streams of information. In a translation model, the decoder generating French queries the encoder's representation of the English source: each French position asks "which English words are relevant to me right now?" Cross-attention is also how multimodal models let text attend to image patches, or how a model conditions generation on a retrieved document.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  subgraph Self["Self-attention"]
    A[sequence X] -->|Q,K,V all from X| B[contextualized X]
  end
  subgraph Cross["Cross-attention"]
    C[decoder seq Y] -->|Q from Y| E[output]
    D[encoder seq X] -->|K,V from X| E
  end
```

The mechanics are identical — same softmax, same scaling. Only the wiring of where queries versus keys/values originate distinguishes the two. That uniformity is part of why the transformer is so composable: the same primitive serves both for a sequence to think about itself and for one sequence to consult another.

## 18.4 — Multi-head attention

A single attention operation forces every position to mix all its relationships into one weighted average. But "it" might need to track *both* the grammatical subject *and* the verb it belongs to — different kinds of relationship that a single averaging step would muddle together. **Multi-head attention** solves this by running several attention operations in parallel, each with its own learned $W^Q, W^K, W^V$ projections.

Each **head** projects the input into a lower-dimensional subspace (typically $d_{\text{model}}/h$ dimensions for $h$ heads), performs scaled dot-product attention there, and produces its own output. The heads' outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear projection $W^O$ that mixes them back together:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O$$
$$\text{where } \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\, K W_i^K,\, V W_i^V)$$

The intuition is division of labor. Different heads specialize: in trained language models, some heads track syntactic dependencies, some attend to the previous token, some follow coreference, some look for matching brackets or quotes. Because each head works in its own subspace, they can attend to different positions for different reasons simultaneously, and the final projection blends those distinct findings.

Note the parameter budget stays roughly constant: splitting $d_{\text{model}}$ into $h$ smaller heads means each head is cheaper, and the total work is about the same as one full-width head — you get diversity of attention patterns essentially for free.

::: {.callout-warning}
More heads is not automatically better. Past a point, heads become redundant and many can be pruned after training with little loss. Heads also each get a smaller subspace, so a very large $h$ can leave each head too low-dimensional to represent useful matches.
:::

## 18.5 — Positional encodings

Attention has a curious blind spot: it is **permutation-equivariant**. Because it computes a weighted average over a *set* of positions, shuffling the input tokens just shuffles the outputs the same way — the mechanism itself has no notion of order. Yet "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" must not be the same. We have to inject position information explicitly.

The original transformer used **sinusoidal positional encodings**: a fixed vector added to each token's embedding, built from sines and cosines at geometrically spaced frequencies:

$$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \qquad PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$

Each dimension is a wave of a different wavelength, so each position gets a unique fingerprint, and — because shifting a sinusoid by a fixed offset is a linear operation — relative offsets are expressible, helping the model generalize to distances and even lengths it did not see in training.

A simpler alternative is **learned positional embeddings**: just allocate a trainable vector per position, like a vocabulary indexed by slot number. This often matches or beats sinusoidal in-distribution, but it cannot extrapolate beyond the maximum length seen in training.

The dominant modern choice is **Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)**. Instead of *adding* a position vector, RoPE *rotates* the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to their absolute position. Because the dot product of two rotated vectors depends only on the difference of their rotation angles, the attention score between positions $m$ and $n$ depends only on the **relative** distance $m-n$. This bakes relative position directly into the score, works well at long context, and underlies most current LLMs.

| Scheme | How it injects order | Extrapolates to longer? | Where seen |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sinusoidal (additive) | fixed sin/cos added to embedding | partially | original Transformer |
| Learned (additive) | trainable vector per slot | no | early BERT/GPT |
| RoPE (rotary) | rotate Q,K by angle ∝ position | yes (with tricks) | most modern LLMs |

## 18.6 — The transformer block

A transformer is a stack of identical **blocks**, each combining two sublayers: a multi-head attention layer and a small **position-wise feed-forward network** (FFN). The attention sublayer mixes information *across* positions; the FFN then processes each position *independently*, applying the same two-layer MLP (typically expanding to $4\times$ the width with a nonlinearity, then projecting back) to every token. Loosely: attention decides *what to gather*, the FFN decides *what to do with it*.

Two ingredients make deep stacks of these trainable. **Residual connections** wrap each sublayer — the sublayer's output is *added* to its input — so gradients have a direct highway back through the network and each layer only has to learn a refinement rather than a full re-encoding. **Layer normalization** rescales each token's vector to stable statistics, keeping activations well-conditioned. Modern transformers favor the **pre-norm** arrangement, normalizing *before* each sublayer ($x + \text{Sublayer}(\text{LayerNorm}(x))$), which trains far more stably at depth than the original post-norm design.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  In[token vectors + positional info] --> LN1[LayerNorm]
  LN1 --> MHA[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
  In --> Add1((+))
  MHA --> Add1
  Add1 --> LN2[LayerNorm]
  LN2 --> FFN[Feed-Forward MLP]
  Add1 --> Add2((+))
  FFN --> Add2
  Add2 --> Out[to next block]
```

Stacking dozens of these blocks gives the model depth: early layers tend to capture local, syntactic patterns; later layers compose them into abstract, semantic representations. The same block design scales from small models to the largest LLMs essentially unchanged — only width, depth, and head count grow.

## 18.7 — Encoders, decoders and causal masking

The original transformer had two halves. An **encoder** stack reads the entire input at once with fully bidirectional self-attention — every token sees every other token, past and future — producing a rich contextual representation. A **decoder** stack generates output one token at a time and adds two twists: a cross-attention sublayer (querying the encoder, as in §18.3), and a *masked* self-attention that may only look at tokens already generated. Three architectural families descend from this:

- **Encoder-only** (e.g. BERT-style): bidirectional, great for understanding tasks like classification and retrieval where the whole input is available.
- **Decoder-only** (e.g. GPT-style): causal, generates text left-to-right; the dominant design for modern LLMs.
- **Encoder–decoder** (e.g. T5-style): encode a full input, decode a transformed output; natural for translation and summarization.

The crucial trick for generation is **causal masking**. When training a decoder to predict the next token, we feed it the whole target sequence at once for efficiency — but we must prevent each position from peeking at future tokens it is supposed to predict. We enforce this by setting the disallowed entries of the score matrix to $-\infty$ *before* softmax, so their attention weight becomes exactly zero:

```python
import numpy as np
n = 4
scores = np.random.randn(n, n)
mask = np.triu(np.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(bool)   # True = future positions
scores[mask] = -np.inf            # block attention to the future
w = np.exp(scores - np.nanmax(np.where(mask, -np.inf, scores), 1, keepdims=True))
w /= w.sum(1, keepdims=True)
assert np.allclose(np.triu(w, k=1), 0)   # no weight leaks forward in time
```

This single masking step is what lets a decoder-only transformer be trained on every position's next-token prediction in parallel while still behaving, at inference, like a strict left-to-right generator.

## 18.8 — Why it beat RNNs, and the quadratic cost

Recurrent networks process a sequence step by step: hidden state $t$ depends on hidden state $t-1$. That sequential dependency has two fatal costs. First, it is **inherently serial** — you cannot compute step 50 before step 49 — so it under-uses parallel hardware like GPUs. Second, the path connecting two distant tokens runs through every token between them, so the gradient signal decays (the vanishing-gradient problem), making long-range dependencies hard to learn.

Self-attention fixes both. Every position is computed **in parallel** — the whole $QK^\top$ matrix is one matrix multiply — and the **path length between any two positions is $O(1)$**: "it" connects to "animal" directly, regardless of distance. This combination of parallelism and short paths is the core reason transformers train faster and model long-range structure better, and it is what let them scale to the enormous sizes behind modern LLMs.

The price is **quadratic complexity**. Computing all pairwise scores for a sequence of length $n$ costs $O(n^2)$ in both compute and memory: doubling the context length quadruples the attention cost. For short sequences this is irrelevant, but for long documents or high-resolution images it becomes the dominant bottleneck. A large research effort attacks this:

| Approach | Idea | Example flavor |
|---|---|---|
| Sparse attention | each token attends to a fixed subset (local window + a few globals) | Longformer, BigBird |
| Low-rank / kernel | approximate softmax attention with linear-cost factorization | Linformer, Performer |
| IO-aware exact | keep $O(n^2)$ math but tile to avoid materializing the full matrix in slow memory | FlashAttention |

FlashAttention is worth singling out: it does not approximate anything — it reorders the computation to stream through fast on-chip memory, giving the exact same result far faster and with far less memory. Much of the practical extension of context windows came from this kind of systems-level engineering rather than a new attention formula.

::: {.callout-tip}
When someone says a transformer "scales to long context," ask *which* lever: an approximate-attention variant (cheaper but lossy), an exact IO-aware kernel like FlashAttention (same result, faster), or a positional scheme like RoPE that extrapolates. They solve different parts of the same problem.
:::

## 18.x — Key takeaways

- Attention is content-based soft retrieval: each position forms a **query**, every position offers a **key** and a **value**, and the output is a softmax-weighted average of values — $\text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling keeps dot-product magnitudes in check so softmax does not saturate and kill gradients.
- **Self-attention** contextualizes a sequence with itself; **cross-attention** lets one sequence query another. **Multi-head** attention runs several in parallel so different heads capture different relationships.
- Attention is order-blind, so **positional encodings** (sinusoidal, learned, or rotary/RoPE) inject sequence order; RoPE dominates modern LLMs because it encodes relative position and extrapolates.
- The **transformer block** = multi-head attention + position-wise FFN, each wrapped in a residual connection and layer norm (pre-norm for stability). Stacking blocks gives depth.
- **Causal masking** ($-\infty$ before softmax) turns a decoder into a left-to-right generator trainable in parallel. Encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder–decoder are the three architectural families.
- Transformers beat RNNs through **parallelism** and **$O(1)$ path length**, at the cost of **$O(n^2)$** attention — addressed by sparse, low-rank, and IO-aware (FlashAttention) variants.

## 18.y — See also

- **Chapter 17 — Recurrent & Sequence Models**: the predecessor architecture and the long-range limitations attention was designed to overcome.
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models**: how decoder-only transformers are scaled, pretrained, and deployed.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment**: adapting pretrained transformers to tasks and human preferences.
- **Chapter 16 — Convolutional Neural Networks**: the alternative inductive bias for spatial data, increasingly fused with or replaced by attention.
- **Chapter 34 — Multimodal AI**: cross-attention as the bridge between text, vision, and audio streams.
- **Chapter 43 — AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference**: KV-caching, FlashAttention, and the systems work that makes attention practical at scale.
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks**: residual connections, normalization, and the optimization machinery that makes deep transformer stacks trainable.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 17 · 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models](ch17.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [19 · 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning →](ch19.html)
